updates.title: Basalingappa v. Mudibasappa
updates.summary: Reiterated that the accused can rebut the Section 139 presumption by raising a probable defense regarding the complainant's financial capacity to lend.

updates.title: Arjun Panditrao Khotkar v. Kailash Kushanrao Gorantyal
updates.summary: Clarified that a Section 65B(4) certificate is a condition precedent for the admissibility of electronic evidence like WhatsApp and Emails.

updates.title: Aneeta Hada v. Godfather Travels
updates.summary: Held that for corporate liability under Section 141, the company must be made a co-accused along with the responsible directors.

updates.title: P. Mohanraj v. Shah Brothers Ispat Pvt. Ltd.
updates.summary: Clarified that Section 138 proceedings are 'quasi-criminal' and the IBC moratorium under Section 14 applies to the corporate debtor but not to directors.

updates.title: Dashrath Rupsingh Rathod v. State of Maharashtra
updates.summary: Established territorial jurisdiction — complaint lies where cheque was presented and dishonoured, not where drawn or notice sent.

updates.title: Kusum Ingots & Alloys Ltd. v. Pennar Peterson Securities Ltd.
updates.summary: Section 138 is a strict liability offence. Presumption under S.139 is strong and rebuttable only by accused.

updates.title: Meters & Instruments (P) Ltd. v. Kanchan Mehta
updates.summary: Court can grant interim compensation under S.143A even at the stage of summoning.

updates.title: Rangappa v. Sri Mohan
updates.summary: Accused has to rebut presumption under S.139 by raising a probable defence. Mere denial is insufficient.

updates.title: S.M.S. Pharmaceuticals Ltd. v. Neeta Bhalla
updates.summary: Averments in the complaint that the director was in charge of the company are essential and sufficient for summoning.

updates.title: Surinder Singh Deswal v. Virender Gandhi
updates.summary: Deposit under S.148 is mandatory for suspension of sentence in cheque bounce appeals.

updates.title: Krishna Janardhan Bhat v. Dattatraya G. Hegde
updates.summary: Standard of proof to rebut S.139 presumption is preponderance of probability, not beyond reasonable doubt.

updates.title: G.J. Raja v. Tejraj Surana
updates.summary: S.143A is prospective and applies only to offences committed after the amendment came into force on 1 Sept 2018.

updates.title: Laxmi Dyechem v. State of Gujarat
updates.summary: Dishonour of cheque on the ground of 'stop payment' or 'signature mismatch' still attracts Section 138 if funds were insufficient.

updates.title: M/s Kumar Exports v. M/s Sharma Carpets
updates.summary: Rebuttal of presumption can be done by showing the absence of consideration and debt based on the preponderance of probabilities.

updates.title: Bir Singh v. Mukesh Kumar
updates.summary: Even if a blank cheque leaf is voluntarily signed and handed over, it attracts the presumption under Section 139.

updates.title: A.C. Narayanan v. State of Maharashtra
updates.summary: A Power of Attorney holder can file a complaint and depose, provided they have personal knowledge of the transaction.

updates.title: MSR Leathers v. S. Palaniappan
updates.summary: A payee can present a cheque multiple times during its validity and issue a notice upon any of the dishonours to trigger S.138.

updates.title: C.C. Alavi Haji v. Palapetty Muhammed
updates.summary: An accused who claims non-receipt of the statutory notice can pay within 15 days of receiving the court summons to escape liability.

updates.title: N. Harihara Krishnan v. J. Thomas
updates.summary: Every person must be impleaded as an accused within the statutory limitation period. Belated impleadment is barred.

updates.title: Meters and Instruments Pvt Ltd v. Kanchan Mehta
updates.summary: S.138 proceedings are civil sheep in criminal wolf's clothing. Compounding can be permitted even without the consent of the complainant if adequate compensation is paid.

updates.title: Gimpex Private Limited v. Manoj Goel
updates.summary: Once a settlement agreement is breached, the complainant can proceed with the original S.138 complaint or file a new one on the settlement cheque.
